toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ## Developer Complaint
2. 
3. Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
4. for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
5. while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
6. 
7. We've already verified:
8. - No analyzers are slowing things down
9. - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
10. - The project itself compiles in <500ms
11. 
12. The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
13. project graph on every CLI invocation.
14. 
15. We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
16. command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.
17.

@@ -1,17 +1,17 @@
 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

data.content:
## Analysis

The developer report confirms the root cause: **MSBuild process startup and project re-evaluation overhead** on every `dotnet build` invocation. Visual Studio avoids this because it keeps MSBuild nodes alive in the background.

By default, `dotnet build` sets `/nodeReuse:false`, killing worker nodes after each build. Here are the fixes, from most to least impactful:

### 1. Use `dotnet watch` for the inner loop (best option)
```bash
dotnet watch build
```
Keeps the process alive and rebuilds incrementally on file changes — no startup cost per iteration.

### 2. Enable the MSBuild Build Server (.NET 8+)
Set the environment variable to keep an MSBuild server process warm between CLI invocations:
```bash
export DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1
dotnet build          # first build: normal speed
dotnet build          # second build: much faster
```

### 3. Enable MSBuild Node Reuse
Re-enable the node reuse that CLI disables by default:
```bash
export MSBUILDDISABLENODEREUSE=0
```

### 4. Project-level opt-in (permanent fix)
Add to `SlowCliBuilds.csproj` or a `Directory.Build.props`:
```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <UseSharedCompilation>true</UseSharedCompilation>
</PropertyGroup>
```
This enables the Roslyn VBCSCompiler server, reusing the compiler across builds (VS does this automatically).

---

**TL;DR** — The 2-4s overhead is MSBuild cold-starting every time. Use `dotnet watch build` for edit-build cycles, or set `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` to get VS-like warm builds from the CLI.
data.reasoningText:
The developer reports that `dotnet build` from CLI is slow (2-4 seconds) for incremental builds even when nothing changed, while Visual Studio rebuilds nearly instantly. They've ruled out analyzers and confirmed incremental build targets work correctly. The overhead is from MSBuild process startup and project graph re-evaluation.

The key insight here is that Visual Studio keeps an MSBuild server process running in the background, so it doesn't pay the startup cost on every build. The CLI `dotnet build` starts a new MSBuild process each time.

The main recommendations:

1. **Use the MSBuild Server** (`dotnet build -nodeReuse:true` or set `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` / `MSBuildServerEnabled=true`). 

Starting in .NET 8, MSBuild has a server mode that keeps the process alive between builds, eliminating the re-evaluation overhead—this is the biggest performance win. Beyond that, `dotnet watch` for development keeps the process warm and does incremental rebuilds, while the `-graph` flag can speed up incremental scenarios by using graph-based builds. For .NET 8+, enabling the MSBuild build server is the primary solution.

The key issue is that `dotnet build` defaults to `/nodeReuse:false`, which kills MSBuild worker nodes after each build, whereas Visual Studio keeps them alive. The MSBuild Server feature (experimental in .NET 7, more stable in .NET 8) solves this by either setting the `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER` environment variable or adding `<MSBuildServerEnabled>true</MSBuildServerEnabled>` to the project file. For immediate improvements, `dotnet watch build` keeps the process running and rebuilds on file changes, while enabling node reuse ensures MSBuild workers stay alive between invocations.
